title: Resolution Plans
content:
Session 6: Formulation and finalization of resolution plan [¬ß¬ß30, 31] 
Kalpraj v Kotak Investment Advisors Ltd [2021 | SC | 3J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD entered CIRP; the RP issued five Form Gs (multiple invitations for EOI/resolution plans) ‚Üí the last date for 
submission of plans under the fifth Form G was 08.01.2019. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RAs included: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč KIAL (Kotak Investment Advisors Ltd.) ‚Äď submitted plan on time. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Kalpraj Dharamshi & Rekha Jhunjhunwala ‚Äď natural persons, submitted their resolution plan after the deadline, 
on 27.01.2019. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The RP allowed Kalpraj‚Äôs late submission, and the CoC consciously approved this. CoC also allowed KIAL to submit a 
revised plan. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Ultimately, the CoC approved Kalpraj‚Äôs resolution plan by a thumping majority of 84.36%. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Only Kotak Mahindra Bank (0.97% vote share), related to KIAL, dissented ‚Üí later, court deals with the question of 
holding company and subsidiary company. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Challenges and procedural history: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč KIAL challenged acceptance of Kalpraj‚Äôs late plan before the NCLT ‚Üí rejected. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Writ petition before High Court ‚Üí rejected on grounds of alternative remedy. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Appeal before NCLAT ‚Üí allowed. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The NCLAT decision was then appealed before the Supreme Court by Kalpraj. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč KIAL‚Äôs allegations (material irregularity): 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Accepting a resolution plan after the notified deadline. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Alleged leakage of KIAL‚Äôs resolution plan ‚Üí because Kalpraj‚Äôs plan was allegedly a copy-paste with minor changes. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether KIAL‚Äôs appeals to NCLAT were within limitation? (Application of Section 14, Limitation Act) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether there was waiver/acquiescence by KIAL, estopping it from later opposing Kalpraj‚Äôs participation? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether NCLAT was correct in interfering with the CoC‚Äôs decision approving Kalpraj‚Äôs resolution plan, especially given 
the alleged ‚Äúmaterial irregularity‚ÄĚ under Section 61(3)(ii) IBC? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC Framework 
48 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 30(2) ‚Äď grounds on which the Adjudicating Authority may reject a plan. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 61(3) ‚Äď limited grounds for appeal against approval of a resolution plan; includes ‚Äúmaterial irregularity‚ÄĚ by 
the RP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Commercial wisdom doctrine (Essar Steel, Swiss Ribbons): 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Judicial bodies cannot question commercial wisdom of the CoC beyond Sections 30(2) and 61(3). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CoC's decision-making is presumed to be informed and expert-driven. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Limitation Act, Section 14 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Time spent in bona fide proceedings before a court without jurisdiction can be excluded for computing 
limitations. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Judicial principles highlighted 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Completion timelines (330 days) are not sacrosanct (Essar Steel). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Deference to CoC decisions is paramount, even if there is technical non-compliance. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP‚Äôs actions that are approved by CoC amount to a ‚Äúwhitewash‚ÄĚ (court's deference). 
 
Application 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 1 ‚ÄĒ Limitation 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Appeal limitation under Section 61 IBC: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
30 days from NCLT order; 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Additional 15 days only if NCLAT is satisfied with ‚Äúsufficient cause‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Total outer limit = 45 days (IBC is a special statute, so Limitation Act Schedule does not apply). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Facts: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLT order: 28.11.2019 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Writ filed in Bombay High Court: 11.12.2019 (before certified copy was obtained on 18.12.2019) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Writ dismissed: 28.01.2020 on ground of alternate remedy under Section 61 IBC 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Appeal before NCLAT filed: 18.02.2020 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Application of Section 14 (exclusion of time): 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 14 allows exclusion of time spent in bona fide, diligent proceedings before a court that ultimately 
cannot decide the matter on merits. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
When Section 14 applies, no delay is attributed; limitation remains the statutory period (30 + 15 
days max). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč HERE ‚Üí  
‚Ė†‚Äč
KIAL filed the writ promptly, even before receiving the certified copy. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Writ was based on a legitimate ground ‚Üí breach of natural justice and irregularity in NCLT 
procedure (orders passed by a bench different from the one that heard submissions). 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Therefore, it was not a frivolous or misconceived proceeding before a ‚Äúwrong forum‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Writ petition was fully contested; High Court issued a 32-page order dismissing it only due to an alternate 
remedy being available. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Hence KIAL acted in good faith and with due diligence. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Time spent in bona fide writ proceedings before the High Court must be excluded under Section 14. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
The appeal filed on 18.02.2020 was therefore within the permissible extended limitation under 
Section 61 IBC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 2 ‚ÄĒ Waiver and Acquiescence 
‚ěĘ‚Äč KIAL had participated in the process, submitted revised plans, and did not object contemporaneously. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
KOTAK cannot object on part of KIAL due to the separate legal personality of the holding and the 
subsidiary companies. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
A holding company and its wholly owned subsidiary (WOS) are separate legal entities. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
The holding company does not own the assets of the subsidiary. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Management and control of the subsidiary‚Äôs business lie with its own Board of 
Directors, not with the parent. 
49 
 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Shareholders (including a parent company) have only limited rights: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč right to dividends if declared; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč right to participate in liquidation proceeds; 
‚óÜ‚Äč
right to vote in shareholder meetings. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč However, SC did not rest the decision solely on waiver; the key analysis concerned the CoC‚Äôs approval and 
commercial wisdom. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 3 ‚ÄĒ Whether NCLAT could interfere? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Material Irregularity 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Accepting a late plan can constitute material irregularity under S.61(3)(ii) ‚Üí SC acknowledged this 
prima facie irregularity. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
But, CoC‚Äôs approval overrides all RP actions, including: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
accepting late plan, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
allowing revised plans, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
continuing evaluation 
‚Ė†‚Äč
HERE, CoC approval was 84.36%. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Only dissent: Kotak Bank (0.97%), which is a related party to KIAL ‚Üí weakens challenge. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Commercial Wisdom Doctrine 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Court reaffirmed earlier holdings: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
No authority (NCLT/NCLAT) can consider grounds beyond Sections 30(2) or 61(3) + 
commercial wisdom is non-justiciable. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
CoC is presumed to be fully informed, acts with expert advice, considers viability & 
feasibility. 
‚óÜ‚Äč
Judicial intervention is barred unless there is patent illegality under the statutory 
grounds. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Whitewashing by CoC 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even if RP violated procedural timelines, approval by CoC ‚Äúwhitewashes‚ÄĚ the defect. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
As long as the CIRP timeline under the Code has not expired, accepting a late plan is not fatal if 
CoC approves. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Therefore, NCLAT erred in substituting its own evaluation for that of the CoC. SC set aside NCLAT‚Äôs order. 
 
Conclusion 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Supreme Court reinstated Kalpraj‚Äôs resolution plan as the successful plan. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Procedural lapses by the RP do not justify judicial interference when CoC has approved the action by a substantial majority. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč This case marks significant strengthening of deference to CoC (‚Äúwhitewashing‚ÄĚ). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Reinforces: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Primacy of commercial wisdom of financial creditors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thumping majority lends strong legitimacy to CoC decisions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Complaints by a related party hold diminished weight. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Timelines (330 days) are flexible, especially when CoC itself consciously deviates. 
 
Binani Industries Ltd v Bank of Baroda [2018 | NCLAT] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CIRP & Plans 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CIRP started for Binani Cement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rajputana Properties (RPPL) submitted a plan which CoC approved. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Ultratech submitted a competing plan offering higher value. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Allegations of Discrimination 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Financial Creditors: 
50 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RPPL‚Äôs plan gave full recovery to some financial creditors but much lower recovery to others who were 
similarly placed. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Creditors with identical rights (direct lenders, guarantee-based lenders, foreign branches) were treated 
unequally. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Operational Creditors: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Unrelated OCs received partial payment. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Related-party OCs got nothing. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
IBC does not permit unequal treatment among OCs of the same class. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Here, discrimination was on the basis of voting share.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT & NCLAT Findings 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT rejected RPPL‚Äôs plan for discriminatory treatment. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT examined both plans and held Ultratech‚Äôs plan to be compliant with Section 30(2). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT replaced the CoC-approved plan and directed implementation of Ultratech‚Äôs plan. 
 
Issues:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the RPPL resolution plan was discriminatory in violation of the IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom is non-justiciable even when the plan discriminates between similarly situated 
creditors? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether NCLAT can review the merits of competing resolution plans (Ultratech vs. RPPL)? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the CoC acted contrary to its duties under the IBC and processed documents? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 30(2) IBC 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP to ensure the plan does not contravene any law. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Must provide for payment to OCs not less than liquidation value (but not necessarily only liquidation value). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 25(2)(h) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CoC/RP must invite resolution plans and ensure value maximization. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 53 IBC (liquidation waterfall) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Explanation cannot be used to justify discriminatory treatment in a resolution plan. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Key principle from NCLAT 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Differential treatment between similarly situated creditors is impermissible. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A resolution plan must balance stakeholders while maximizing value. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Underlying objective of the IBC 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Keep CD as a going concern. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Maximize asset value. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Balance stakeholders. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Discrimination Against Financial Creditors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč EXIM and SBI (HK) were similarly situated to other FCs who received 100%. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No justification offered; discrimination arbitrary. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RPPL claimed certain transactions were ‚Äúshady,‚ÄĚ but: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
That is not a basis under IBC to differentiate between equal FCs. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT: RPPL plan violated the principle of equal treatment among equals. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Discrimination Against Operational Creditors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Unrelated OCs paid 35%; related-party OCs paid 0. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT: No regulatory basis for such distinction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Liquidation value cannot be the benchmark for treatment of OCs. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč If OCs only receive liquidation value: 
51 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Future suppliers will stop giving credit, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Hurts functioning of IBC and impacts the broader economy. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CoC‚Äôs Failure of Duty 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT found serious lapses by CoC: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Failed to maximize the value of CD‚Äôs assets. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Failed to properly consider Ultratech‚Äôs superior plan. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Non-application of mind. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The court emphasized multiple times that maximization of value is essential. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Supreme Court‚Äôs later jurisprudence (Essar Steel) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Although Essar Steel (2019) strongly protects CoC discretion, Binani shows the outer limit ‚Üí When CoC‚Äôs 
decision leads to discrimination, NCLAT may intervene. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT‚Äôs Power to Review Competing Plans 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT effectively held Ultratech‚Äôs plan: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Non-discriminatory, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Maximizes value, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Complies with s.30(2). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Declaring it binding on CD ‚Üí this is extraordinary; arguably beyond the standard role of appellate authority. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Nevertheless, justified due to CoC‚Äôs egregious failure. 
 
Conclusion 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RPPL‚Äôs plan was discriminatory against similarly situated FCs and OCs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CoC failed in its duty to maximize value and balance stakeholder interests. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT held that commercial wisdom is not absolute; 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A plan that is discriminatory can be struck down. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This forms the outer boundary of deference to CoC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Ultratech‚Äôs plan was found compliant with s.30(2) and directed to be implemented. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč THIS IS PROBLEMATIC ‚Äď GOES AGAINST COC CW FTW DOCTRINE. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Case remitted to NCLT for execution of Ultratech‚Äôs successful resolution plan. 
 
Kalyani Transco v Bhushan Power [2025 | SC | 3J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč BPSL entered CIRP with massive debt; CoC approved JSW Steel‚Äôs resolution plan, upheld by NCLT/NCLAT. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Kalyani Transco challenged the plan before the Supreme Court on three grounds: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 29A Eligibility 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Allegation that JSW Steel‚Äôs eligibility was not independently verified. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RPs relied only on self-declarations instead of conducting a proper inquiry. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CIRP Timeline 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CIRP had exceeded the statutory outer limit of 330 days ‚Üí time-barred and could not continue. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Discrimination Against OCs 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The plan allegedly gave unfairly low recovery to OCs compared to financial creditors. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Claimed violation of Section 30(2) IBC and Regulation 38, which require equitable treatment for 
similarly situated creditors. 
 
Issues: 
Whether the CoC becomes functus officio once the adjudicating authority approves a plan under section 31. 
 
Held: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Continuation of CoC‚Äôs Existence Beyond Plan Approval 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The CoC does not cease to exist at the moment of approval. 
52 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Its role continues until the resolution plan has been completely implemented and until all judicial or quasi-judicial 
challenges reach finality. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This reasoning flows from: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The statutory scheme of sections 30 and 31. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The need for creditor oversight during implementation. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Prior jurisprudence emphasizing that successful resolution, not mere approval, is the IBC‚Äôs objective. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Accordingly, when the CoC decided to extend the implementation timeline in order to accommodate delays caused 
by extraneous circumstances, this decision could not be struck down on the argument that the CoC lacked 
authority. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Clauses Allowing Extension of Implementation Period Do Not Make the Plan ‚ÄúIndeterminate‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Appellants argued that because the resolution plan allowed for extensions of the timeline for implementation, it 
effectively amounted to a provision for withdrawal or modification of the plan, making it non-compliant with IBC 
provisions and rendering it liable to be rejected. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Supreme Court clarified: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A clause allowing extension of time is not equivalent to a clause permitting withdrawal or modification of 
a plan. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Extensions are commercially necessary and often unavoidable in large, complex transactions. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Such clauses do not violate section 30(2) nor any regulations in force. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, the attack on the plan as ‚Äúindeterminate‚ÄĚ was rejected. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Delay in Implementation Was Due to Extraneous and Unavoidable Circumstances 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Delay was attributable to: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Attachment of BPSL‚Äôs assets by the ED, which created uncertainty and prevented smooth transfer of 
management. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLT orders redirecting EBITDA generated during the CIRP toward interim distribution. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Multiple litigations, including actions initiated by operational creditors and erstwhile promoters. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court emphasized: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
JSW Steel and the CoC acted with diligence and ‚Äúbest efforts‚ÄĚ to expedite implementation. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Delays beyond the control of stakeholders cannot be a ground to invalidate a resolution plan, especially 
after its full implementation. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
This finding aligned with earlier precedents (e.g., Essar Steel, Ghanshyam Mishra). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Priority of Payments to Operational Creditors ‚ÄĒ Compliance With Regulations at the Time 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A major objection was that JSW paid financial creditors ahead of operational creditors, purportedly violating: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
section 30(2)(b) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
regulation 38 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The principle of equitable treatment. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Supreme Court made a critical temporal distinction: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
At the time the BPSL plan was approved (2018), regulations required that operational creditors be paid 
the amounts ‚Äúdue to them‚ÄĚ in priority. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
In BPSL‚Äôs case, the admitted amount due to operational creditors was nil. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Therefore, any ex gratia or good-faith payment to operational creditors could validly be made after 
fulfilling dues owed to financial creditors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč However, the Court expressly refrained from commenting on how this interpretation may change under the 
amended regulations that now require payment of amounts ‚Äúpayable under the resolution plan‚ÄĚ in priority. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Thus, while the plan complied with the regulatory framework as it existed, the ruling leaves open 
questions for future disputes. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CCDs Are Equity Instruments ‚ÄĒ JSW‚Äôs Upfront Equity Infusion Was Valid 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A key challenge was that JSW Steel infused funds through Compulsorily Convertible Debentures (CCDs) rather 
than direct equity and therefore allegedly failed to satisfy the plan‚Äôs equity-infusion requirement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Relying on a long line of precedents (e.g., Narayan Nagappa, Dhoot Developers), the Court reaffirmed: 
53 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CCDs are equity instruments in substance, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Must be treated as equity contributions for the purposes of the resolution plan. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, JSW had fully met its upfront equity obligations. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč EBITDA Generated During CIRP Cannot Be Claimed Later by Lenders 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Another major contention concerned the huge EBITDA generated while BPSL was under insolvency. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court found: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Request for Resolution Plan (RFRP) did not contain any clause specifying how the interim profits 
(EBITDA) should be distributed. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The CoC had earlier taken a stand that EBITDA would be used for running the corporate debtor as a 
going concern. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Allowing a claim to EBITDA at this late stage would ‚Äúrevive settled claims‚ÄĚ and violate the core objective 
of timely resolution under the IBC. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
This reinforces that creditors cannot reopen settled commercial positions years after implementation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Protecting the Integrity of the IBC 
‚ěĘ‚Äč In its concluding observations, the Supreme Court issued a strong warning: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rejecting or disturbing a resolution plan after it has been implemented, and after the company has been revived, 
defeats the entire architecture of the IBC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč BPSL has been modernized by JSW Steel and converted into a major profit-making enterprise employing 
thousands. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Setting aside such a plan would have caused severe disruption not only to the company but also to the broader 
economy and investor confidence. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court stressed the overarching importance of certainty, finality, and deference to CoC‚Äôs commercial wisdom. 
 
Ghanashyam Mishra v Edelweiss Asset [2021 | SC | 3J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CIRP was initiated against OMML on an SBI Section 7 application. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Multiple applicants submitted resolution plans: GMSPL, EARC, etc. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč EARC‚Äôs claim was rejected by the RP because it was based on a corporate guarantee allegedly given by OMML to 
APNRL (a group company); the RP found it unsubstantiated. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CoC approved GMSPL‚Äôs plan with 89% vote. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT and NCLAT dismissed EARC‚Äôs challenge but NCLAT held that rejected claims/statutory authorities could still 
pursue claims after resolution. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Statutory dues of the Government were not included in the plan. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The government attempted to recover dues after approval. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Meanwhile, Parliament amended Section 31(1) to expressly include Central/State Govt authorities among parties bound by 
a resolution plan. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether, after approval under Section 31(1), creditors ‚Äď including government authorities ‚Äď can pursue claims not included 
in the resolution plan? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the 2019 amendment to Section 31(1) (including govt dues) is retrospective? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 31(1) IBC ‚Äď ‚Äúshall be binding‚ÄĚ ‚Äď a resolution plan approved by the AA is binding on: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč corporate debtor, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč employees, members, creditors, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč government authorities, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč guarantors and all other stakeholders. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 2019 Amendment ‚Äď expressly includes Central/State Govt and statutory dues within Section 31(1). 
54 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 238 IBC ‚Äď IBC overrides all inconsistent laws. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Clean Slate Doctrine 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The purpose of resolution is to give the resolution applicant a ‚Äúfresh start‚ÄĚ. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Information Memorandum contains all liabilities ‚Üí RA must know the total burden upfront ‚Äď allowing 
new claims after approval would defeat certainty and discourage bidders. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, all claims not part of the approved plan stand extinguished. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Government is Bound ‚Äď Even Without Amendment 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court held even before the 2019 amendment, statutory dues fell within: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
‚Äúoperational debt‚ÄĚ (s.5(21)), 
‚Ė†‚Äč
‚Äúoperational creditor‚ÄĚ (s.5(20)), 
‚Ė†‚Äč
‚Äúcreditor‚ÄĚ (s.3(10)), 
‚Ė†‚Äč
and at the very least ‚Äúother stakeholders‚ÄĚ in Section 31(1). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, the government was always meant to be bound. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Amendment is Clarificatory and Retrospective 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The amendment merely clarifies an existing position. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Introduced because tax authorities kept issuing demands despite approval of plans. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Clarificatory amendments operate retrospectively. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the Government cannot revive or enforce claims after plan approval ‚Üí even if resolution occurred before 
2019. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Post-Approval Claims Are Barred 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Any claim not included in the Resolution Plan is frozen, extinguished, and unenforceable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No civil proceedings, quasi-judicial actions, tax recovery, or other mechanisms survive. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Once a Resolution Plan is approved under Section 31, all claims not included in it ‚Äď whether of financial creditors, 
operational creditors, statutory authorities, or government ‚Äď stand extinguished. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No person can continue or initiate proceedings for pre-CIRP dues not part of the plan. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The 2019 amendment to Section 31(1) is clarificatory and retrospective. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Therefore, the government and EARC cannot revive or pursue their excluded claims after plan approval.
cases: Kalpraj v Kotak Investment Advisors Ltd
Binani Industries Ltd v Bank of Baroda
key_principles: ‚Äč CD entered CIRP; the RP issued five Form Gs (multiple invitations for EOI/resolution plans) ‚Üí the last date for
‚Äč KIAL (Kotak Investment Advisors Ltd.) ‚Äď submitted plan on time.
